title: Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita, 2024
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national economic output and the typical daily resources available to individuals, plotting GDP per capita on the horizontal axis against median income or consumption per day on the vertical axis. Both axes use a logarithmic scale measured in international dollars at 2021 prices, allowing for comparison across a wide range of economic development levels. The data points are color-coded by region, with North America in orange, South America in maroon, Africa in purple, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue, while the size of each circle corresponds to the country's total population. A strong positive correlation is visible across the chart, where higher GDP per capita consistently aligns with higher median daily income or consumption. The United States sits at the top right, showing the highest values on both measures, exceeding $50,000 in GDP per capita and roughly $60 in median daily resources. Japan and European nations cluster in the upper middle range, with GDP per capita between $30,000 and $50,000 and median daily figures between $20 and $50. Large Asian economies like China and India occupy the middle ground; China shows a GDP per capita near $20,000 with a median around $15 to $20, while India sits lower with GDP per capita under $10,000 and median daily resources around $5 to $10. At the lower end of the distribution, countries such as Burundi, DR Congo, and Madagascar display GDP per capita below $2,000 and median daily income or consumption under $2. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time, and depending on the country and year, the median data relates to income measured after taxes and benefits or to consumption per capita. The visual pattern shows a tight grouping of points along an upward diagonal, indicating that as national wealth increases, the median individual's daily economic standing rises in tandem.
